speaker: interviewer
text: And we're in Sydney, Australia. Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's on again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Leonie, you were telling me about how your husband asked for your hand in marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had-- that was-- we couldn't find a normal synagogue, and we had to go to a very strict religious synagogue where I have to go around my future husband about three times, or whatever. You know, with all the customs, that was a very-- and the women had not so much say as they have today in, in those days. I can't remember the year I got married, but i was about four weeks in Singapore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me something. Were you a proper-- a full bride, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bride. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bride. In a white dress and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a honeymoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't remember. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you know where you went to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Singapore. We had a little house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a little house?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and the suburb was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Mr. Penhaus remain your friend or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a reas-- a decent man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very, very nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: His brother came to see me here in Sydney years ago, years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's lovely. So you and your husband were having a happy relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he worked as an engineer, and he was, I think, sounds quite affluent man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would have had to help.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to the perfect-- you have to have help in Singapore, otherwise, you lose face.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so you were enjoying life? Life was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. Wherever-- look-- as they photographed me wherever I went, I was taking this this way, look that way. Mrs. Oppenheim-- My name was Oppenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And his name was? His first name? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leo Oppenheim. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Leonie. And he was Leo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two Leons.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did his fam-- he have family there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was alone there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where had he been born? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harbin.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Harbin. In Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was originally Russian. But he was a graduate of the Hong Kong University.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. But your common language was English?

speaker: interviewee
text: English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your English started to improve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, Yes Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And got better. OK. And how long were you married? Or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came there in '39? About-- and when did the war broke out? '42? '43? In Singapore, the bombs were falling, and the two ships were sunk-- the Repulse and the other one was name-- that's the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was sunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was sunk. And do you know what? The, the Japs came through from the north, K-- Kota Bharu, and how they got there through the jungle was just nak-- nearly naked. And German officers were there too. They came through too, and Singapore fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was lucky to make the boat. Women and children were evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He, he, he had to join the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia. And were you very upset about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know when or ever if you were going to see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never saw him again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the English government wrote me that he was killed in action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, dear. So there you were, a young widow, and very unhappy again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So that was quite a thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here as a young widow. No, I heard here. In 1945, I heard. In 1945-- I, I came here in '42. We landed in '42. From Singapore, the boat came via Fremantle and down to Sydney. And I begged-- in Fremantle, everybody had to get off. But I had an idea. I said to the captain, look, Captain, and I will find a job in Sydney. I hear you're going to Sydney. Please let me come. And he said, all right, if you pay. I said, I promise you I pay my fare after I find a job. And so I did. Every week, I sent so and so many pounds, and it was paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Singapore fell, you came straight to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now Singapore fell-- it didn't fall then when we were evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So what happened then to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Singapore fell while there was a-- well, I was evacua-- after a few days, I worked in the Cross. [NON-ENGLISH] Holy Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Red Cross. They put me to make sandwiches. I mean, we all did something. The bombs were falling around us daily. And women and children were evacuated in Singapore. I don't think anybody was left there, you know? And on board the ship, the Japs attacked us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Attacked the women and the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boat. We had a big Red Cross flag over the boat, and we were still attacked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they attack you? They beat you or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they throw bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- so you were having--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were very lucky we got through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you'd lost-- you were separated from your husband and being bombed. And did you have friends on board the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, friends. People from Singapore who lived there always, the women and children. And I can tell you a funny story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? Well that was a boat for the troops, a troop. And downstairs was the mess-- was a mess table where you set for dinner or whatever meal you took. That was downstairs-- very primitive, simple boat, and no weapons or anything like that on board. And we had one doctor not another oh, and the crew. That was all. And the mess tables were-- and you know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Long, narrow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Long, narrow. And we had the mattress on that. And that was our bed. And you know, while the bombs were falling, the boat going this way and that way at night. And everybody was supposed to go upstairs. But I slept so well, I fell down, but I went back. And I fell down again. And I went back. And everybody else was already on board, with on top, with jackets and what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I slept. I slept through the whole business. And in the morning, and, and it was-- the attack was finished, they said, are you crazy? We wanted you to come, and we couldn't wake you. You were sleeping so fast, and it was so noisy. How could you sleep through? But I slept through, through the attack of the Japs. That was lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And [LAUGHS] unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in the Fremantle. And everybody had to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you persuaded them to bring you to Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really-- you came to Sydney knowing no one? Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a soul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were very lucky that that man took your word that you would pay him back. He felt sorry for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very gorgeous. Yes, the captain. Yes. I said to him, I will. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you docked in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell me what happened. I went with another girl, and I don't know where we went to. Yeah. And we looked for a flat at the Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still had some money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them that we will eventually get some money from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took you on--

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Different times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had-- we used-- we rented a little house, a little unit. It's still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the Cross in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as busy as it is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was probably a better area in a sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a good area, but it was full with young people, mostly young.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were young yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you fitted in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so were you constantly--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked-- and we both-- this girl I shared the flat with and I, we went looking for a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you assume-- did you think in your head you were going to see your husband again or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought you would get together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It was 1942. In '45, [INAUDIBLE]. That was three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And so, what did you get a job, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had difficulties. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well you had no English, no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very poorly. And no money. But we had-- we had established-- we got-- I got five, five pounds a, a week. I got five pounds a week from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Malaysian government. They established because quite a number were came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a considerable about in those days. Was that a fair amount?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was quite good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got a job also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found a job. You have an accent? We can't use you. People who won't understand you. That was David Jones. I went to Farmers, same story. You have an accent. We don't want that. You might not be able to, to do the job. Until I come to Mark Foy's. I don't know if any of you--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know Mark Foy's very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Beautiful shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Anyway, it was before your time. There was a very nice-looking woman who was a buyer for garments. And this woman looked at me and she said, yes, tell me-- I am friendly with a man from Germany. His name is-- he's unfortunately dead. I've forgotten his name now. His-- we were in the same tennis club. But I've forgotten his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It will come. Yeah, I knew him. And he said, do you know so and so? And I said, yes. We played tennis together. He was also a very good tennis player. But he didn't live in Berlin. But I played as his-- in a mixed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Doubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed double. I played with him in, in-- not in Singapore. In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he said, you are here? And so on. So I joined here the tennis club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bondi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's still existing, I think. I don't know if--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, there is a one. The Maccabi, is that it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- it wasn't Maccabi at the time. It was a Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it's in Wellington Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Well-- Wellington Street, yeah. And I joined there. But I had different jobs. Anyway, that girl, Mona--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Mark Foy's.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Mark Foy's. Was head buyer-- a very smart looking girl, really. She was in love with that-- was that man. He wasn't married then either. Unfortunately, my memory is bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. So then you had other jobs as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a few jobs where I didn't stay. But I stayed with-- in Mark Foy's for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you still lived at the Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then I remembered-- we're still living at the Cross. The girls-- there were two other girls as well. Their husbands came and-- by boat, by, by a rowing boat. How they got there, they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they found them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found-- they found their husbands. And I was left alone. Well, I couldn't afford alone the flat. And I told that to Mona from Mark Foy's. And I remembered a name I was given in Singapore. If I ever come to Sydney, go and see that family. And that family, I had the name. I rang up, and they said, yes, come and see us because my son is just moving out. That was in Randwick, a unit. And you can have his room. Anyway, I went to see those people. John even, I think, knows them. And they gave-- I paid 30 shillings for board and lodging.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were good people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they looked after you nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like family. They were absolutely gorgeous to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. And how long were you with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with them quite a while-- a few years. And then-- where did I move to? Yeah. Then some of my-- I met some Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got-- I get-- pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never go to synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not really? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were meeting with-- mixing with Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And also, the tennis. And where was I working? I was-- I've forgotten where I worked at the time. But I had a few jobs. I was never very steady. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you? Were you at Randwick when you heard that your husband-- had got the letter from the British government, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told by the British government that he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can show you the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this very devastating for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't very pleased, naturally. I didn't know John then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I met John-- I don't remember when-- yeah, '47 I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how-- remember how you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to ask him. No. Wait a minute. Where did-- on the tennis courts, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the tennis courts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. On the tenn--

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot's happened on the tennis courts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS] On the tennis courts, he was watching me. I was playing a competition. He was watching me and giving me advice. And that's it. [INAUDIBLE] Who is that? [LAUGHS] And so that was it. And then you married in-- do you know? Oh, no, not-- took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So two years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years later. He came in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- where had John come from? Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he-- what was his profession? Or what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Profession, he had established himself with his brother. They had a little office [? hole. ?] And he invented-- or didn't invent-- you know those pullovers? Winter pullovers, this double knit or something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean a skivvy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a skivvy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he had a design put on them. And so old women would stitch that for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to do his correspondence. My English was then already much better. And it's even better than what it is today. And so that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and John?

speaker: interviewee
text: With John, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was 8 and 1/2 months in bed with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your pregnancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my pregnancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So that wasn't a very happy pregnancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] It was happy. I mean, I had to lie down because I was losing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had Jeffrey.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had Jeffrey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jeff-- Jeffrey-- what does Jeffrey do today?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he's doing today?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a Queen's Counselor.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a wonderful profession.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he's a very, very clever boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's married and has twins. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who we're going to meet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll be going to meet his twins later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So-- all right. Do you now think about all those, those war years a lot? Do you-- does your mind go back to that? Not really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No, not so much. But now I'm getting-- I will be 80. And I'm really getting old now. I can feel it. I can't do this. I can't do that. I forget this, and I forget that. I mean, I'm not in pain like my other friends. They have back aches and other aches. I have no aches, only here. The head is not very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I believe that you have been a very, very good bridge player.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm not a good bridge player. Here are my trophies.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you s-- you must have been. I can see a room full of trophies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm not a good bridge player, but I have good partners to play with. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still play bridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you still go for walks and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I still play golf too, but very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And the tennis days are over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tennis is over long time because I did not know how beautiful is golf. It is no comparison to, to tennis. No comparison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you're out with the trees and nature.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how often do you play a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belong to a club which is called Bonnie Doon.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know Bonnie Doon well.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know Bonnie Doon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't say. Have you-- do you play?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I know a gentleman who used to be the head of-- Brian Bromberger. And I think he's the captain. He's the head of it, or president, or something. But I don't play golf, not yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bromberger, is that the Jewish man?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're a bit anti-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Brian Bromberger is Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is he?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's there now, yeah? You wouldn't know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so. He's, he's a-- he's a lawyer, and he's a academic. So I know him. All right. Well, we're going to meet your family soon. And thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we are, Leonie, with all your beautiful family around you. Now I'm going to ask them to introduce themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are John.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- the question?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, you just--

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you introduce yourself please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see. I am John Hilton. John Julius Hilton. And my family is sitting here. That's my wife, Leonie. That my son, Jeffrey, and my daughter-in-law of Susie, and the two grandchildren. One is Emily, as you can see. And the other one is Gemma. And 4 and 1/2 years of age, these little ones. And it's a very, very happy family when we are together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now Emily or Gemma, do you want to say-- do both of you want to say something? All right. Now, Emily. Just Emily? Right. Emily, what do you-- what would you like to say about your grandma? Your nanny? Lonny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lonny.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say about Lonny? Is she--

speaker: interviewee
text: [MUMBLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say that a bit louder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's beautiful. And would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gemma, come on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You're shy.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did she say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you want to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: She did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just say it a bit louder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just say it a bit louder. How about you tell mommy? Loudly? What do you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a secret. I'll tell you when we get home. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then you can't be in the camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now I just wanted to ask Jeffrey, if I might. Jeffrey, I'd like to know, please, how you feel that your parents different war experiences may have impacted upon you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think in many ways, my parents war experiences impacted upon me less than similar war experiences might have impacted upon others. Because I think that when I was growing up, there was actually very little concentrated discussion about it. It was not a subject that was discussed or that was mentioned from time to time very briefly, but there was no kind of systematic discussion. And I would have to say that I did not have a very detailed knowledge of that experience. So that they saw it as their task that I should endeavor to lead what they regarded as normal a life as possible in their new homeland of Australia. And they thought that would be achieved by my-- by it not being discussed very much, and by their trying not to bring it up with me. And in some ways, I think whilst I admit that it externally, didn't have a great impact, I think there's part of it that I carried around with me inside, which I never completely understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. So would you say that your-- you feel it is a good thing that your parents have told-- each have told their story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really do for this reason-- that I'm not-- I haven't listened. I've haven't seen it. I'm looking forward to seeing it one day. And I can guarantee you that a very large amount of what they've talked about, I do not know. And I'm 43 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's something for posterity. And for your children to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it because you had your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is. Because I had my birthday. And it's also something that I haven't able to explain to the girls because I don't know it myself is any serious systematic way. I think it'll be very good for them, that they should know about it. Because I think it's very important because it shouldn't ever happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Susie, are you also pleased that they have told their story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am I think it's very important for everybody that's been through the experience to tell their story. I'd like to add that I think that having-- both of them having gone through this experience, that they are people who show no discrimination to someone like myself, who is not Jewish, and has married into this family, and been made perfectly welcome. And I love them both for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mom, don't cry. Don't cry. You are crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't cry, Mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thank-- Mr. And Mrs. Hilton, thank you very much for telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just got very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we're all crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two of the interview with Leonie Hilton. It's the 7th of February 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
